title: Carrot and coriander soup
subtitle: Sweet roasted carrots spiced with cumin and finished with fresh coriander.
excerpt: Carrots roasted to bring out their sweetness, then simmered with cumin and ground coriander and blended smooth. A spoonful of crème fraîche or a swirl of olive oil at the bowl is the only finish it needs. Ready in under an hour.
sourceNotes: Carrot and coriander soup became one of the most widely made soups in British home cooking through the 1980s and 1990s, driven partly by the vegetarian food movement and partly by the availability of ground coriander as a pantry standard. The recipe is British in origin (drawing on the tradition of spiced vegetable broths) though the spicing owes something to Indian cookery reaching the British kitchen.

content.text: Carrot and coriander soup works because carrots are naturally sweet enough to carry the spice without any help from cream or butter. Sweating the carrot low and slow brings out that sweetness; the coriander and cumin add warmth without heat. This is a light, clean soup that eats well in any season.

The recipe uses ground coriander in the base and fresh coriander at the finish. They taste quite different: the dried spice adds earthiness deep in the soup; the fresh herb brightens it at the surface. Both matter.
A large saucepan and a stick blender are the main kit. A heavy-based pan helps the onion sweat without catching.

Warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the onion and a good pinch of salt. Cook for 8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and translucent. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and the grated ginger if using, and cook for another 2 minutes.

Add {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin and {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant. Add the carrots and stir to coat in the spiced oil. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock and bring to a simmer.

Cook at a steady simmer for 30 to 35 minutes until the carrots are completely tender when pierced. Remove from the heat and blend until smooth with a stick blender. Taste and adjust seasoning. If the soup is thicker than you like, add a splash more stock. Ladle into bowls and finish with fresh coriander and a spoonful of crème fraîche if using.

items.fix: Add a squeeze of lemon juice to brighten it. For the next batch, keep the heat medium-low when frying the spices.
items.cause: Ground coriander or cumin scorched during the 30-second frying stage.

items.fix: Add hot water or stock and blend again briefly.
items.cause: Simmered with too little stock, or stock reduced heavily.
items.symptom: Soup is too thick after blending

items.fix: Ensure carrots are fully soft before blending. Replace ground coriander if it has been in the cupboard for more than a year.
items.cause: Carrots simmered rather than properly cooked through, or old ground spices.
items.symptom: Colour is dull orange rather than bright

items.fix: Take the onion all the way to golden and slightly sweet before adding the spices. The 8-minute minimum is the floor, not the target.
items.cause: Onion not sweated for long enough.

content.text: Carrot and coriander became one of the defining soups of British vegetarian cooking in the 1980s. By the 1990s it was on every wholefood café menu and every homemade soup list in the country. The appeal is simple. Carrots are cheap, plentiful, and sweet. Coriander is the spice that makes an ordinary vegetable feel thought-through. The soup is now so woven into British home cooking that it barely reads as a recipe any more.
